candidates.label: Litigation Practice
candidates.definition: Matters that include official proceedings (e.g., court case, arbitration, mediation)

candidates.label: Trial Practice
candidates.definition: Trial Practice refers to the set of legal skills and procedures involved in presenting a case before a court of law, including witness examination, jury selection, trial preparation, testimony, and motion practice.

candidates.label: Service
candidates.definition: The legal work performed, usually by a Legal Services Provider, in the course of a legal matter.

candidates.label: Contract Law
candidates.definition: The General Theory of Contracts is an umbrella term used in civil law jurisdictions, like Spain and Latin America, that refers to the body of law that governs contracts. In the U.S. and other common law jurisdictions, the equivalent would simply be Contract Law or Law of Contracts. They both concern principles and rules that regulate agreements made between parties, detailing how contracts are formed, executed, and potentially breached, and what remedies are available.

candidates.label: Agreements
candidates.definition: Agreements are written documents that establish a legally binding understanding between two or more parties, outlining the terms and conditions of a transaction or relationship.

candidates.label: Service Agreement
candidates.definition: A Service Agreement is a written document that outlines the terms and conditions of a service provided by one party to another.

candidates.label: Contract & Quasi-Contract Claims
candidates.definition: Contract & Quasi-Contract Claims refer to legal claims arising from the breach of a contract or an agreement that is not legally binding, but where one party has received a benefit that would be unjust to retain without compensation.
synonyms: 契約および準契約請求

candidates.label: Rescission
candidates.definition: Rescission is the act of canceling or voiding a contract, typically due to factors such as duress, unconscionability, or fraud, and is a type of equitable relief available in contract and quasi-contract claims.

candidates.label: Consumer Contract Claims
candidates.definition: Consumer Contract Claims are legal claims made by a party in a contract with a consumer, including claims related to billing errors, unlawful provisions, unconscionable circumstances, and improper actions by loan brokers.

candidates.label: Quasi-Contract Claim
candidates.definition: A quasi-contract claim is a legal claim that arises when one party receives a benefit from another party, even though there was no actual contract between them, and the law implies a promise to pay for that benefit.

candidates.label: Contract Law
candidates.definition: The General Theory of Contracts is an umbrella term used in civil law jurisdictions, like Spain and Latin America, that refers to the body of law that governs contracts. In the U.S. and other common law jurisdictions, the equivalent would simply be Contract Law or Law of Contracts. They both concern principles and rules that regulate agreements made between parties, detailing how contracts are formed, executed, and potentially breached, and what remedies are available.

candidates.label: Breach of Contract
candidates.definition: The concept of Breach of Contract refers to the failure of one party to fulfill their obligations as outlined in a legally binding agreement, resulting in harm or damages to the other party.

candidates.label: Breach of Contract Claims
candidates.definition: A breach of contract claim is a legal action taken by one party against another for failing to fulfill their obligations as outlined in a contractual agreement.
synonyms: Actions en cas de violation de contrat

candidates.label: Consumer Contract Claims
candidates.definition: Consumer Contract Claims are legal claims made by a party in a contract with a consumer, including claims related to billing errors, unlawful provisions, unconscionable circumstances, and improper actions by loan brokers.

candidates.label: Repudiatory Breach of Contract
candidates.definition: A Repudiatory Breach of Contract Claim is a legal action asserting that one party has committed a breach so fundamental that it effectively nullifies the contract, allowing the innocent party to terminate the agreement. This type of claim alleges that the breach goes to the root of the contract, depriving the innocent party of substantially the whole benefit they were intended to derive from it.

candidates.label: Anticipatory Breach of Contract
candidates.definition: Anticipatory Breach of Contract refers to a situation where one party to a contract indicates, before the time for performance, that they will not perform their obligations under the contract.

candidates.label: Tortious Breach of Contract
candidates.definition: The Tortious Breach of Contract is a legal concept that occurs when one party breaches a contract and causes harm or damages to the other party, resulting in a tort claim in addition to a breach of contract claim.
synonyms: Violação contratual com responsabilidade civil extracontratual
不法行為に基づく契約違反

candidates.label: Contract & Quasi-Contract Claims
candidates.definition: Contract & Quasi-Contract Claims refer to legal claims arising from the breach of a contract or an agreement that is not legally binding, but where one party has received a benefit that would be unjust to retain without compensation.
synonyms: 契約および準契約請求

candidates.label: Violation
candidates.definition: A violation is an act or instance of breaking or failing to comply with a rule, regulation, contract, or law, which can lead to legal consequences.

candidates.label: Rescission
candidates.definition: Rescission is the act of canceling or voiding a contract, typically due to factors such as duress, unconscionability, or fraud, and is a type of equitable relief available in contract and quasi-contract claims.

candidates.label: Quasi-Contract Claim
candidates.definition: A quasi-contract claim is a legal claim that arises when one party receives a benefit from another party, even though there was no actual contract between them, and the law implies a promise to pay for that benefit.

candidates.label: Tortious Interference Claims
candidates.definition: Tortious Interference Claims refer to legal claims made by a party against another for intentionally interfering with their contractual or business relationships, resulting in economic harm or loss.

candidates.label: Claims Agent
candidates.definition: A claims agent is a person who acts on behalf of an individual or organization to manage and process insurance claims.
synonyms: クレームエージェント

candidates.label: Contract Law
candidates.definition: The General Theory of Contracts is an umbrella term used in civil law jurisdictions, like Spain and Latin America, that refers to the body of law that governs contracts. In the U.S. and other common law jurisdictions, the equivalent would simply be Contract Law or Law of Contracts. They both concern principles and rules that regulate agreements made between parties, detailing how contracts are formed, executed, and potentially breached, and what remedies are available.

candidates.label: Intervenor Claim
candidates.definition: An Intervenor Claim is a legal pleading filed by a third party who seeks to join an ongoing lawsuit because they have a direct interest in the outcome of the case.

candidates.label: Claim of Exemption
candidates.definition: A claim of exemption is a legal document filed in bankruptcy cases that allows a debtor to protect certain property from being seized by creditors.

candidates.label: Proof of Claim
candidates.definition: Proof of Claim: A document filed in a bankruptcy case by a creditor or claimant asserting a right to payment from the debtor's assets.

candidates.label: Claims Adjusting
candidates.definition: This U.S. industry comprises establishments primarily engaged in investigating, appraising, and settling insurance claims.

hierarchy_path.label: Insurance Carriers and Related Activities Industry
Agencies, Brokerages, and Other Insurance Related Activities

candidates.label: Breach of Contract Claims
candidates.definition: A breach of contract claim is a legal action taken by one party against another for failing to fulfill their obligations as outlined in a contractual agreement.
synonyms: Actions en cas de violation de contrat

candidates.label: Breach of Contract
candidates.definition: The concept of Breach of Contract refers to the failure of one party to fulfill their obligations as outlined in a legally binding agreement, resulting in harm or damages to the other party.

candidates.label: Break Violation Claim
candidates.definition: A break violation claim is a legal action initiated by an employee against their employer relating to the legal requirement to provide breaks during the workday. These breaks can include rest breaks and meal breaks, and the claim typically arises when an employer either denies or interrupts breaks or does not provide them in accordance with applicable labor laws. In some cases, employers may also be liable for break violations if they do not compensate employees for certain types of work performed during breaks.

candidates.label: Violation
candidates.definition: A violation is an act or instance of breaking or failing to comply with a rule, regulation, contract, or law, which can lead to legal consequences.

candidates.label: Compliance
candidates.definition: Deontic compliance refers to the conformity in action, behavior, or attitude to a set of rules or standards, as prescribed by laws, regulations, or ethical principles.

candidates.label: Antitrust and Competition Law
candidates.definition: Law surrounding business competition.
synonyms: Derecho de la Competencia y Antimonopolio
Droit de la concurrence et antitrust
独占禁止法及び競争法

candidates.label: Compliance
candidates.definition: Deontic compliance refers to the conformity in action, behavior, or attitude to a set of rules or standards, as prescribed by laws, regulations, or ethical principles.

candidates.label: Antitrust - Bid-Rigging Claims
candidates.definition: A claim made under antitrust law alleging that multiple parties colluded to manipulate the bidding process in order to artificially inflate prices.
synonyms: Antimonopolio - Reclamaciones de colusión en licitaciones
Antimonopolio - Reclamaciones de manipulación de licitaciones
Antitrust - Accusations de collusion à l'appel d'offres
Antitruste - Alegações de Conluio em Licitações
反垄断 - 投标串通指控
独占禁止法 - 入札不正請求の主張

candidates.label: Antitrust - Pricing Claims
candidates.definition: Antitrust - Pricing Claims refer to allegations of anti-competitive behavior related to pricing practices, such as price-fixing or predatory pricing, in violation of antitrust laws.
synonyms: 独占禁止法 - 価格請求

candidates.label: Antitrust - Predatory Pricing Claims
candidates.definition: Antitrust - Predatory Pricing Claims refer to allegations of anti-competitive behavior by a company that sets prices below cost with the intention of driving competitors out of the market.
synonyms: 反垄断 - 掠夺性定价索赔
競争法 - 捕食的価格請求

candidates.label: Compliance
candidates.definition: Deontic compliance refers to the conformity in action, behavior, or attitude to a set of rules or standards, as prescribed by laws, regulations, or ethical principles.

candidates.label: Litigation Practice
candidates.definition: Matters that include official proceedings (e.g., court case, arbitration, mediation)

candidates.label: Service
candidates.definition: The legal work performed, usually by a Legal Services Provider, in the course of a legal matter.

candidates.label: Securities Fraud
candidates.definition: The wrongful act of deceiving or manipulating investors in the securities market, resulting in financial loss or harm.

candidates.label: Fraud
candidates.definition: Fraud is the intentional act of deceiving someone in order to gain an unfair advantage or cause harm.

candidates.label: Insider
candidates.definition: A statutorily created role, where that Insider (e.g., C-suite executive, activist shareholder) can influence the Debtor. Relevant Statute: 11 USC 101(31).

candidates.label: Insider Report
candidates.definition: An Insider Report is a document filed by company insiders, such as executives or directors, to disclose their trades in company stock. This report is required by securities regulators to ensure transparency and prevent insider trading.

candidates.label: Insider Trading
candidates.definition: The act of buying or selling securities based on material, non-public information obtained from a company or its insiders, in violation of a duty to keep such information confidential.

candidates.label: Consumer Protection Law
candidates.definition: Law that creates a more equitable balance for buyers in the marketplace and prevents sellers from using dishonest tactics.
synonyms: Loi sur la protection des consommateurs

candidates.label: Unfair Competition Claims
candidates.definition: Unfair Competition Claims refer to legal actions taken by businesses against competitors who engage in deceptive or unfair practices that harm their business, such as trademark infringement, trade secret misappropriation, or deceptive trade practices.

candidates.label: Unfair Competition Claims
candidates.definition: Unfair Competition Claims refer to legal actions taken by businesses against competitors who engage in deceptive or unfair practices that harm their business, such as trademark infringement, trade secret misappropriation, or deceptive trade practices.

candidates.label: Matter Resolution
candidates.definition: A matter's resolution — the point at which the legal matter is resolved. Some Providers refer to this as a "pencils down" period. This event may differ from `Matter Closure`, which may occur later.

candidates.label: Dispute Resolution Clause
candidates.definition: A Dispute Resolution Clause is a provision in a contract that outlines the process for resolving any disagreements or conflicts that may arise between the parties involved, typically through negotiation, mediation, or arbitration.

candidates.label: Dispute Service
candidates.definition: A process that describes a conflict/adversarial event potentially or actually involving a second party. This can include government entity or internal investigation

candidates.label: Service
candidates.definition: The legal work performed, usually by a Legal Services Provider, in the course of a legal matter.

candidates.label: Arbitration Practice
candidates.definition: Arbitration, a form of alternative dispute resolution (ADR), is a way to resolve disputes outside the courts. The dispute will be decided by one or more persons (the "arbitrators", "arbiters" or "arbitral tribunal"), which renders the "arbitration award".

candidates.label: Service
candidates.definition: The legal work performed, usually by a Legal Services Provider, in the course of a legal matter.

candidates.label: Mediation Practice
candidates.definition: Mediation is a process wherein the parties meet with a mutually selected impartial and neutral person who assists them in the negotiation of their differences. Mediation leaves the decision power totally and strictly with the parties.

candidates.label: Service
candidates.definition: The legal work performed, usually by a Legal Services Provider, in the course of a legal matter.
